title: Engineering Delocalization in Graphene Nanoribbons via Quasiperiodic Edges and Electronic Interactions
authors: Diego B. Fonseca, Anderson L. R. Barbosa, Luiz Felipe C. Pereira
abstract: We investigate localization effects in zigzag graphene nanoribbons with quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type edge extensions, accounting for electron-electron interactions. We employ a tight-binding model that includes first- and third-nearest-neighbor hoppings, in which electronic interactions are treated within a self-consistent mean-field Hubbard approximation. Charge transport properties are calculated using the Landauer-Büttiker formalism. Our results reveal that the combination of quasiperiodic geometry and electronic interactions gives rise to nontrivial transport phenomena. Specifically, the system exhibits three transport regimes: in the non-interacting case, we observe geometric localization. For weak interactions, the system shows a conductive regime with transmission oscillations, whose multiplicity increases with the Fibonacci generation order. In this regime, delocalization emerges from the interplay between geometry and interaction-induced correlations. Finally, for strong interactions, repulsion dominates, and the system returns to a localized state. Our results demonstrate that quasiperiodic edge engineering, combined with electronic interaction control, offers a promising path to modulate transport in graphene nanoribbons.
summary: This paper demonstrates that electronic interactions can be used to engineer transport in graphene nanoribbons by suppressing localization caused by quasiperiodic edge patterns. It reveals a unique three-regime transport behavior where weak interactions promote delocalization while strong interactions restore a localized state. This provides a potential mechanism for controlling conductance through edge engineering and interaction control.
main_problem: Investigating how the interplay between quasiperiodic edge geometry (Fibonacci-type extensions) and electron-electron interactions drives localization and delocalization in graphene nanoribbons.
main_result: The study identifies a transition from localized to delocalized to localized transport regimes as the Hubbard interaction strength increases, demonstrating that weak interactions can suppress geometric localization.
method: Self-consistent mean-field Hubbard approximation combined with the Landauer-Büttiker formalism and Green's function-based numerical simulations using KWANT.
model_or_system: Fibonacci edge-extended zigzag graphene nanoribbons (FEE-ZGNRs) modeled by a tight-binding Hamiltonian including first- and third-nearest-neighbor hopping and on-site Coulomb repulsion.
key_observables: Charge transmission coefficient (T), conductance, local density of states (LDOS), and multifractal characteristics (Generalized Hurst exponent and singularity spectrum).
important_parameters: Hubbard interaction strength (U), Fibonacci generation order (S1-S6), hopping amplitudes (t1, t3), and Fermi energy (E).
main_assumptions: Use of a mean-field approximation for electron-electron interactions and a spin-symmetric reduction assuming equal spin densities; calculations are performed at zero temperature in the linear response regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Fibonacci edge geometry; Figure 2 compares transmission for different hopping models; Figure 5 shows transmission decay vs. block number; Figure 7 displays LDOS spatial distribution; Figure 8 is a heat map of the transport transition; Figure 9 presents multifractal analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Fibonacci edge-extended GNR system, details the mean-field Hubbard model and transport formalism, presents results on transport regimes and the role of third-neighbor hopping, analyzes the microscopic localization mechanisms via LDOS, and concludes with a multifractal analysis of the transmission.
why_interesting: The paper explores the competition between structural disorder (quasiperiodicity) and many-body correlations, a fundamental theme in understanding how interactions can tune localization-delocalization transitions in low-dimensional systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of a pristine zigzag nanoribbon, as well as the first six generations of Fibonacci edge-extended ribbons.

FIG. 2. Main panels show charge transmission as a function of Fermi energy and number of blocks, for several generations of FEE-ZGNRs shown in Figure 1. (a) Charge transmission considering only first-nearest-neighbors (t3 = 0) and (b) with first- and third-nearest-neighbors (t3 = t1/10). Insets on the left-hand-side show the region around E = 0. (c) Charge transmission at E = 0 considering only first-nearest-neighbors (t3 = 0) and (d) with first- and third-nearest-neighbors (t3 = t1/10). Insets in (c) and (d) expand the region around 1,2 and 3 blocks.
FIG. 3. Illustration of the first four generations of Fibonacci edge-extended zigzag graphene nanoribbons with multiple block repetitions: N = 1, 2 and 3.

FIG. 4. Charge transmission through the different genera- tions of FEE-ZGNRs depicted in Figure 1, as a function of Hubbard interaction U at energy E = 0. (a) Only first- nearest-neighbors (t3 = 0) and (b) first- and third-nearest- neighbors (t3 = t1/10).

FIG. 5. Charge transmission (symbols) through the different generations of FEE-ZGNRs depicted in Figure 3, as a function of the number of blocks at energy E = 0 and Hubbard interaction U = 0.1t1, 0.2t1, 0.5t1, and 1.0t1 in each column from left to right. For (a-d) only first-nearest-neighbors are considered (t3 = 0), while in (e-h) first- and third-nearest-neighbors (t3 = t1/10) are included. Solid lines are guides to the eyes.
